synthesis_text: To isolate the 502/504 spikes, first correlate the error timestamps with DB CPU and query‑latency metrics (N1:1:758d9f92) and enable end‑to‑end tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) on the gateway and backend to see per‑downstream‑call latency (N6:0:2112c43e, N1:0:159d53d6). Instrument each downstream call with explicit timeouts and circuit‑breaker logic (N2:5:9a3b54f3) and verify the breaker settings open on repeated timeouts (N6:4:fb74dce0). Check that the DB connection‑pool size matches the DB’s max connections and isn’t causing saturation (N2:4:5d799155). Use pg_stat_statements to identify the highest‑CPU queries (N2:0:c150fd92) and profile/optimise them, adding indexes where needed (N1:4:6fd99091). If overload is confirmed, either scale read replicas or increase the pool size (N1:5:cb23e187, N7:5:48c59627). Adjust per‑call timeout budgets to be lower than the gateway timeout so failures fail fast (N6:7:4614df12). Run targeted load tests to isolate whether latency originates in the network path or the database processing (N8:5:6156d5c6) and, as a validation step, gradually reduce concurrency or apply stricter circuit‑breaker limits to see if the error rate drops (N10:4:9867a1fd).

First, correlate the 502/504 spikes with DB CPU and query duration metrics (N1:1). Enable end‑to‑end request tracing (OpenTelemetry) on the gateway and backend (N1:0, N6:0) to break latency into per‑dependency components and establish timeout budgets (N10:0). Use p95/p99 latency of each downstream call to pinpoint the slowest service (N10:3). Run a targeted load test against the backend to reproduce the CPU spike and measure downstream call durations (N6:3). Profile the backend code to detect serialized calls and parallelize independent calls (N6:5). On the database side, enable pg_stat_statements to identify the highest‑CPU queries (N2:0) and add missing indexes or optimize statements, possibly increasing the connection pool size (N6:6). Refactor inefficient queries to reduce result size and eliminate N+1 patterns (N2:6). After applying these fixes, monitor metrics and adjust circuit‑breaker or timeout settings as needed.

To isolate the intermittent 502/504 after the release, first correlate the gateway error spikes with database CPU and query latency (N1:1:758d9f92). Enable comprehensive DB monitoring – CPU, active sessions, lock waits and pg_stat_statements (N7:0:fbff1b27, N2:0:c150fd92) – and generate a top‑CPU query report (N7:1:50bc5ac4). Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the hottest statements (N2:1:16f3035a) and add missing indexes on filter/join columns to eliminate sequential scans (N2:2:6e9a3561). Inspect pg_locks for lock contention or deadlocks introduced by recent changes (N7:2:4a44d277). Verify that the application connection‑pool size does not exceed the database’s max connections and adjust or scale read replicas if the pool is saturating the DB (N7:4:04a63f6a). Instrument distributed tracing for each downstream call to pinpoint latency contributors (N6:0:2112c43e) and set per‑call timeouts lower than the gateway timeout so failures fail fast and trigger retries (N6:7:4614df12).

To isolate the cause of intermittent 502/504 after the release, first enable end‑to‑end request tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to measure latency of each downstream call. Correlate the timestamps of 502/504 spikes with DB CPU and query duration metrics. On the database side, enable pg_stat_statements and run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the hottest statements, adding missing indexes or refactoring queries to reduce CPU load. Verify that the backend connection‑pool size matches the DB max connections and is not exhausted; adjust pool size if needed. Check the load balancer health‑check results and monitor its request‑queue depth for spikes that align with the errors. Use p95/p99 latency of each downstream dependency to pinpoint the slowest component. Finally, ensure per‑call timeouts are lower than the gateway timeout and configure circuit‑breakers with exponential backoff so that overloads fail fast, reducing cascading 502/504 responses.

To isolate the cause of intermittent 502/504 errors after the release, first enable end‑to‑end request tracing (e.g., X‑Request‑ID or OpenTelemetry) on the gateway and backend to measure latency of each downstream call [N3:3:fe1d8bd1]. Collect TCP layer statistics such as retransmits, RTT and SYN‑retry counts on both sides to detect network congestion [N8:0:3dfac69c] and review kernel TCP counters for timeout clues [N3:6:4f71dac8]. Capture packet traces between the gateway and downstream services to spot loss or high latency [N8:1:b9e78084]. Verify that the backend’s database connection‑pool size matches the DB’s max connections and is not exhausting resources [N2:4:5d799155]; if pool exhaustion is observed, adjust the pool or increase DB capacity. Profile the database: run EXPLAIN ANALYZE, check pg_stat_statements and add missing indexes on filter/join columns to eliminate sequential scans that drive CPU [N2:2:6e9a3561, N1:4:6fd99091]. Correlate the timestamps of 502/504 spikes with DB CPU and query duration metrics to confirm whether query load is the trigger [N1:1:758d9f92]. Apply load‑reduction techniques: gradually lower request concurrency or introduce circuit‑breaker limits on downstream calls to see if error rates drop [N10:4:9867a1fd], and implement circuit‑breaker or exponential‑backoff retries to prevent cascading failures [N3:8:76a23887]. This systematic tracing, network diagnostics, DB tuning, and protective throttling will pinpoint the bottleneck and stabilize the service.
